title: 2 Samuel 13
subtitle: Amnon, Tamar, and Absalom: The Sword Begins

sections.header: Verses 1–22 — Amnon Rapes Tamar; David Does Nothing

heb.paragraph: A key Hebrew term in this section on Jonadab's scheme; the locked door; "hatred was greater than the love"; David was furious but... carries theological weight illuminating 2 Samuel's interplay of covenant promise, royal failure, and divine faithfulness.

refs.note: The Davidic covenant is the theological backbone of 2 Samuel. Every episode — victory, sin, punishment, restoration — is read in the light of God's unbreakable promise to David's house.
"Jesus Christ the son of David." The NT opens by placing Jesus in David's lineage — everything in 2 Samuel points forward to the king who will sit on David's throne forever.

notes.note: MacArthur: The passage on Jonadab's scheme; the locked door; "hatred was greater than the love"; David was furious but... reveals God's sovereign management of David's kingdom — blessings and judgments alike serve the covenant purpose. The Davidic covenant (ch 7) is the theological backbone of the entire book; every episode is read in its light.

MacArthur: David is the Bible's most fully developed human character — warrior, poet, lover, sinner, penitent, king. His complexity is not a literary weakness but a theological asset: God works through flawed people, not around them.

MacArthur: The messianic trajectory runs through every chapter of 2 Samuel. David's throne, David's seed, David's city — all are types of Christ's eternal kingdom. "The LORD declares to you that the LORD himself will establish a house for you" (7:11).

MacArthur: The practical application is both comforting and sobering: God's promises are unbreakable, but his discipline is real. David kept his throne but lost his peace. Grace does not eliminate consequences; it sustains the sinner through them.

Calvin: On Jonadab's scheme; the locked door; "hatred was greater than the love"; David was furious but...: God's providence governs every detail of David's reign — victories, sins, and consequences alike serve the divine purpose. David is simultaneously the anointed king and a broken sinner, and God uses both dimensions to accomplish his will.

Calvin: The church must learn from David that no height of spiritual privilege exempts one from the consequences of sin. The sword that never departed from David's house (12:10) is a warning to every generation that presumes upon grace.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: The Hebrew syntax here employs thewayyiqtolnarrative chain with strategically placed disjunctive clauses that provide background information and shift perspective. Key institutional terms —melek,nāgîd,māšîaḥ— carry distinct political and theological connotations.

NET Note: Textual variants between MT and 4QSamᵃ/LXX in this section affect the narrative's details though not its theological direction. The NET translation notes indicate where critical decisions between readings were necessary.

bergen.source: Robert D. Bergen, 1, 2 Samuel, New American Commentary (1996) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Bergen: On Jonadab's scheme; the locked door; "hatred was greater than the love"; David was furious but...: the narrative advances 2 Samuel's central theological argument — God's covenant with David (ch 7) is both unconditional in its promise and consequential in its outworking. David's sins do not void the covenant but unleash its disciplinary clauses.

Bergen: The narrator's literary skill is on full display — irony, foreshadowing, and the interplay between public kingship and private failure create the most psychologically complex character portrait in the OT.

anderson.source: A.A. Anderson, 2 Samuel, Word Biblical Commentary (1989) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Anderson: On Jonadab's scheme; the locked door; "hatred was greater than the love"; David was furious but...: the text-critical evidence for this passage reveals occasional divergences between MT, LXX, and 4QSamᵃ. The philological analysis illuminates the narrator's craft and the institutional vocabulary of the early monarchy.

Anderson: The Hebrew syntax and vocabulary here are characteristic of the Succession Narrative's prose style — psychologically realistic, politically nuanced, and theologically restrained. The narrator shows rather than tells, leaving moral evaluation largely to the reader.

hist.context: This section addresses Jonadab's scheme; the locked door; "hatred was greater than the love"; David was furious but.... The narrative advances 2 Samuel's central tension: the Davidic covenant is unconditional in its promise but consequential in its outworking. David's victories, sins, and sufferings all serve the larger story of God building a kingdom through a flawed but chosen king.

sections.header: Verses 23–39 — Absalom Murders Amnon; Flees to Geshur

heb.paragraph: A key Hebrew term in this section on two years of waiting; the sheep-shearing feast; Absalom's calculated revenge; three years in exile carries theological weight illuminating 2 Samuel's interplay of covenant promise, royal failure, and divine faithfulness.

refs.note: The Davidic covenant is the theological backbone of 2 Samuel. Every episode — victory, sin, punishment, restoration — is read in the light of God's unbreakable promise to David's house.
"Jesus Christ the son of David." The NT opens by placing Jesus in David's lineage — everything in 2 Samuel points forward to the king who will sit on David's throne forever.

Calvin: On 39 — Absalom Murders Amnon; Flees to Geshur: : God's providence governs every detail of David's reign — victories, sins, and consequences alike serve the divine purpose. David is simultaneously the anointed king and a broken sinner, and God uses both dimensions to accomplish his will.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: On 39 — Absalom Murders Amnon; Flees to Geshur: ced disjunctive clauses that provide background information and shift perspective. Key institutional terms —melek,nāgîd,māšîaḥ— carry distinct political and theological connotations. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: King of Israel; man after God's heart
ppl.text: David dominates chapter 13, displaying the complex mixture of faith, political skill, and moral failure that makes him the OT's most fully developed character — and the most important type of Christ.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThen she took the pan and served him the bread, but he refused to eat. “Send everyone out of here,” Amnon said. So everyone left him.ESVAnd she took the pan and emptied it out before him, but he refused to eat. And Amnon said, “Send out everyone from me.” So everyone went out from him.NIVNow Amnon had an adviser named Jonadab son of Shimeah, David’s brother. Jonadab was a very shrewd man.ESVBut Amnon had a friend, whose name was Jonadab, the son of Shimeah, David’s brother. And Jonadab was a very crafty man.

src.title: Tel Dan Stele (c.840 BC)
src.quote: The Aramaic inscription mentioning “the house of David” is the earliest extra-biblical reference to David's dynasty.
src.note: Confirms the historical existence of a Davidic royal house, corroborating the 2 Samuel narrative of dynasty-founding.

src.title: Mesha Stele / Moabite Stone (c.840 BC)
src.quote: King Mesha of Moab describes his revolt against Israelite control of Transjordan, referencing the “house of David.”
src.note: The Moabite perspective on Israelite territorial claims illuminates the geopolitical context of David's Transjordan campaigns in 2 Samuel 8-10.

src.title: Ancient Near Eastern Royal Ideology and Succession Narratives
src.quote: Egyptian, Hittite, and Mesopotamian court narratives describe succession crises, palace intrigues, and the legitimation of new dynasties.
src.note: The Succession Narrative (2 Sam 9-20) has been compared to Egyptian and Hittite court literature for its psychological realism and political sophistication.

rec.text: Augustine reads David's story as a parable of grace — the greatest king is also the greatest sinner, and his restoration through repentance (Ps 51) becomes the paradigm for every believer's return to God.

rows.label: Amnon Rapes Tamar; David Does Nothing — 13:1-22
Resolution
rows.text: Covenant promise and royal consequence intertwine through divine sovereignty

A key Hebrew term in this section on two years of waiting; the sheep-shearing feast; Absalom's calculated revenge; three years in exile carries theological weight illuminating 2 Samuel's interplay of covenant promise, royal failure, and divine faithfulness.

thread.target: The LORD looks at the heart
thread.text: 2 Samuel 13 continues the exploration of David's heart — the quality that made him king and the vulnerability that made him fall.
The Davidic covenant finds its ultimate fulfilment in Jesus, the Son of David who inherits the eternal throne.

tx.title: Important Qumran witness for 2 Samuel
tx.content: 4QSamᵃ preserves readings closer to the LXX than the MT in several passages, including significant variants in 2 Sam 5-6 and the appendices (chs 21-24).
tx.note: The textual evidence confirms that the MT of 2 Samuel, like 1 Samuel, is not always the best text. Critical editions now routinely consult 4QSamᵃ alongside LXX.

tx.title: First-century retelling of David's reign
tx.content: Josephus's account smooths over some of David's moral failures while expanding the political narrative, revealing how Second Temple Judaism received the David tradition.
tx.note: Josephus confirms the broad historical outline while showing interpretive tendencies in the reception of the David story.

themes.note: 2 Samuel 13: the tension between God's unconditional promise and David's conditional experience shapes every episode. Grace does not eliminate consequences; it sustains the sinner through them.
